content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kim Delfino 
2060 8th A venue 
Sacramento, CA 95818 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-055 
Dear Ms. Delfino: 
May 15, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act {the "Act").1 Please note that our advice is based solely on 
the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-government 
employment law. Also, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices 
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re 
Oglesby {1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
FACTS 
You recently left your appointed position as a commissioner of the Water Commission. 
While serving on the Water Commission you were, and continue to be, employed by the California 
branch of the Defenders of Wildlife, a national nonprofit organization focusing on wildlife and 
habitat conservation. Your position is California Program Director on water issues. While you were 
a commissioner, Defenders of Wildlife refrained from talcing positions on California water storage 
issues. Now that you have left the Water Commission, your employer would like you to work on 
such issues, including regulations to be adopted under Proposition I. 
A general circulation newspaper has asked you to write an editorial on Proposition I. Also, 
you recently received a telephone message from one of the current commissioners asking you to 
return his call. You have not done so pending receipt of our advice. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions 
are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references arc to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 oftbe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of tbe California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


One-Year Ban 
File No. A-15-055 
Page No. 2 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation18746. l(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date 
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban 
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person a<; an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(3) and (4).) 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific 
parties such as •judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance 
or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or 
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation 
of a permit, Jicense, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 
18746.l(b)(S).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made 
for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication 
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "which budget, 
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official 
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and control of the 
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve 
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6).) 
Permanent Ban 
The "p_ermanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of 
absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741. l(a)(l).) 


File No. A-15-055 
Page No. 3 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or 
any oral or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency .... " (Section 87400(c).) 
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she 
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, 
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of 
confidential information .... " (Section 87400(d).) 
Question 1. Appearing before and communicating with the Water Commission regarding: 
(a) Water storage issues generally 
We do not have sufficient facts regarding your proposed communications to know if the 
revolving door bans apply. However, generally: 
One-Year Ban 
The "one-year ban" prohibits you from making, on your employer's behalf, appearances 
before or communications with the Water Commission for the purpose of influencing 
administrative or legislative action. You will need to determine whether an appearance or 
communication is prohibited by the one-year ban on a case-by-case basis by applying the above 
rules to the pertinent facts. 
Pennanent Ban 
The permanent ban applies to appearances and communications that are made with the 
intent to influence judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceedings in which an official participated. 
"Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest 
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency. Presumably, as a commissioner you deliberated and voted on such matters. Therefore, you 
are permanently barred from appearing before or communicating with the Water Commission with 
the intent to influence, or aid, advise, counsel, consult, or assist in representing any other person in 
such proceedings in which you participated. 


(b) Regulations to be adopted under Proposition 1 
One-Year Ban 
File No. A-15-055 
Page No. 4 
The "one-year ban" applies to appearances and communications made for the purpose of 
influencing administrative or legislative action. Actions or proceedings involving regulations are 
administrative actions. Therefore, for a period of twelve months from the date you left the Water 
Commission, you may not appear before or communicate with (whether in person, by telephone or 
correspondence) the Water Commission for the purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, 
modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding involving the regulations. 
Pemzanent Ban 
Adoption of regulations is not a judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding. Accordingly, you are 
not prohibited by the permanent ban from participating in such proceedings. 
(c) The awarding of bond funds for particular water projects 
One-Year Ba11 
The one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the purpose of 
influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving 
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale 
or purchase of goods or property." Thus, influencing the awarding of bond funds for particular 
water projects would be prohibited for one year. 
Pem1anellt Ban 
You indicate that during your tenure as a commissioner, no bond funds had been awarded. 
Thus, there have been no judicial or quasi-judicial proceedings regarding the awarding of bonds in 
which might have participated. Assuming you did not otherwise participate in the other aspects of 
the Water Commission's award of the funds (such as setting specifications for the award, etc.), you 
are not prohibited by the permanent ban from participating in such proceedings. 
Question 2. Writing a newspaper editorial about Proposition 1 
One-Year Ban 
Regulation 18704.2 expressly permits communications with the general public or the press. 
(Regulation 18704.2(b)(2).) The newspaper editorial is a communication addressed to the general 
public and, therefore, is not prohibited by the one-year ban. 
Permanent Ban 
You would not be participating in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding by writing a 
newspaper editorial and, therefore, you may write the editorial without violating the permanent ban. 


File No. A-15-055 
Page No. 5 
Question 3. Speaking with individual commissioners 
One-Year Ban 
Social conversations with commissioners or employees of the Water Commission that are 
not aimed at influencing the administrative or legislative actions of the Water Commission are not 
prohibited by the one-year ban. (Tobias Advice Letter, No. A-96-089.) Therefore, you may speak 
with individual commissioners so long as you are not doing so to influence legislative or 
administrative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or 
property. 
Pen11ane11t Ban 
You would not be violating the permanent ban by speaking with a commissioner so long 
you do not intend to influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which you 
participated. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

participated.
